elements.sent-2411-001: Recent works on Generalized Referring Expression Segmentation (GRES) struggle with handling complex expressions referring to multiple distinct objects.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is because these methods typically employ an end-to-end foreground-background segmentation and lack a mechanism to explicitly differentiate and associate different object instances to the text query.
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we propose \archname, a method that incorporates object-level reasoning into the segmentation process.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our model leverages both text and image inputs to extract a set of object-level tokens that capture both the semantic information in the input prompt and the objects within the image.
elements.sent-2411-005: By modeling the text-object alignment via instance-level supervision, each token uniquely represents an object segment in the image, while also aligning with relevant semantic information from the text.
elements.sent-2411-006: Extensive experiments on the gRefCOCO and Ref-ZOM benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly advances state-of-the-art performance, setting a new standard for precise and flexible GRES.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/Teaser_Nov12.pdf} \caption{(a) Previous GRES methods typically output a single foreground mask in an end-to-end manner, struggling with complex cases involving multiple referred object instances. In contrast, our proposed method automatically localizes relevant object instances associated with different parts of the input prompt before aggregating them to produce the final mask (b).} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-007: Referring Expression Segmentation (RES) aims to segment the objects described by an input language expression.
elements.sent-2411-008: Standard RES approaches~\cite{mao_generation_2016, ding_vision-language_2021, wang_cris_2022, huang_referring_2020 mainly focus on single-target scenarios with the input expression describing a single object, e.g., ``the kid in red''.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Generalized Referring Expression Segmentation (GRES) broadens this scope to handle more complex cases, including expressions that refer to multiple objects, such as ``all people,'' or cases where the input image may not contain any valid target.
elements.sent-2411-010: To handle GRES, recent works~\cite{liu_gres_2023, shah_lqmformer_2024, hu_beyond_2023, luo_hdc_2024 utilize transformer-based architectures to improve the multimodal interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-011: ReLA~\cite{liu_gres_2023 models the complex interactions among regions and the dependencies between different parts of the image and words in the expression.}
elements.sent-2411-012: LQMFormer~\cite{shah_lqmformer_2024 addresses query collapse and performs query selection through a multi-modal query feature fusion technique.}
elements.sent-2411-013: HDC~\cite{luo_hdc_2024 enhances the decoding process through a hierarchical scheme with object counting to model complete semantic context.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite these advances, all these methods share a common limitation: they all follow an end-to-end approach that predicts a single foreground-background mask and lack an explicit mechanism to differentiate and associate individual object instances with specific parts of the text query.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, they struggle with complex prompts referring to multiple distinct objects, leaving such cases as an open challenge.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, when asked to segment ``the girl who holds a small dog and the dog on the right'' in the image shown in Fig. \ref{fig:teaser , most methods either segment all girls and dogs indiscriminately or get inaccurate segments, as observed with the state-of-the-art GRES method ReLA~\cite{liu_gres_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Thus, we need a model to explicitly predict and link relevant objects to specific semantic entities of the input text, to accurately segment each object described in complex expressions.
elements.sent-2411-018: To this end, we introduce \archname, a novel GRES model that incorporates instance-level reasoning into the segmentation process.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our approach is simple but effective; we adapt the instance segmentation framework~\cite{cheng_per-pixel_2021, cheng_masked-attention_2022, li_mask_2022 to identify and segment only object instances described in the prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-020: To facilitate this, we design a novel loss function to constrain each segmented object to explicitly link to specific semantic entities of the input text.
elements.sent-2411-021: Thus, \archname~ learns to parse both the input text and images into small components to establish explicit connections between the input expression and the corresponding objects in the image.
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically, we integrate text prompt inputs in a query-based instance segmentation architecture, Mask2Former \cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022 , and guide this model to identify and segment only object instances in the image that correspond to the input expression.}
elements.sent-2411-023: To achieve this, we ensure that each segmented object is precisely linked to the specific semantic component (i.e., phrase) of the input text describing it.
elements.sent-2411-024: We enforce this through our proposed Phrase-Object Alignment loss function, which explicitly identifies the text phrase from the input expression that best corresponds to each segmented object and strengthens this association.
elements.sent-2411-025: This alignment ensures that the model not only identifies and segments the correct objects but also captures the fine-grained relationships between textual phrases and visual instances.
elements.sent-2411-026: Further, we propose two modules to adapt the instance segmentation framework to the GRES setting: an Adaptive-Instance-Aggregation (AIA) module and a no-target prediction head.
elements.sent-2411-027: AIA automatically integrates all segmented object instances based on their relevance scores to predict the final mask.
elements.sent-2411-028: This enhances the model's focus on the most relevant instances, thereby improving segmentation performance and robustness in complex GRES scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-029: The no-target prediction head aggregates both text and object instance features to accurately predict the target-absent cases.
elements.sent-2411-030: We evaluate \archname~ on two widely used benchmarks for GRES: gRefCOCO~\cite{liu_gres_2023 and Ref-ZOM~\cite{hu_beyond_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Our experiments demonstrate that \archname~significantly outperforms state-of-the-art GRES methods and LLM-based approaches~\cite{lai_lisa_2024,chen_sam4mllm_2024,xia_gsva_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The results highlight \archname's ability to handle complex referring expressions, setting a new standard in the field.
elements.sent-2411-034: We propose {\archname , the first instance-aware approach to GRES that effectively handles complex multi-object scenarios by explicitly differentiating and associating individual object instances with specific phrases in the text.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We introduce a phrase-object alignment mechanism, enabling our model to link each segmented object with the corresponding semantic part of the input text.
elements.sent-2411-036: We develop an Adaptive Instance Aggregation module that dynamically integrates segmented object instances based on their relevance scores, improving overall segmentation performance.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard GRES benchmarks, significantly improving segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-038: On gRefCOCO, \archname~ achieves a gIoU of $74.34\%$ and N-acc of $79.72\%$, outperforming previous methods by $3.3\%$ and $12.25\%$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-039: The vision community has widely adopted Transformer-based architectures~\cite{xu_bridging_2023, su_language_2023, yang_lavt_2022, chng_mask_2024, liu_referring_nodate, huang_referring_2023, kim_restr_2022, tang_contrastive_2023, liu_polyformer_2023, shang_prompt-driven_2024, wu_towards_2023 to solve RES task.}
elements.sent-2411-040: VLT~\cite{ding_vision-language_2021 uses cross-attention to produce vector queries from multimodal features.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Similarly, LAVT~\cite{yang_lavt_2022 shows that early cross-modal fusion of features improves alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-042: CRIS~\cite{wang_cris_2022 leverages CLIP's robust image-text alignment capabilities to perform pixel-text contrastive learning.}
elements.sent-2411-043: VATEX~\cite{nguyen-truong_vision-aware_2024 decomposes language cues into object and context understanding and leverages vision-aware text features to improve text understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Recently, ReMamber~\cite{yang_remamber_2024 integrates the power of Mamba to explicitly model image-text interaction while Mask Grounding~\cite{chng_mask_2024 explicitly learns the correspondence between text and visual tokens.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: ReLA~\cite{liu_gres_2023 introduces a region-based GRES baseline that models the relationship both among different image regions and between regions and words.}
elements.sent-2411-046: DMMI~\cite{hu_beyond_2023 introduces two decoder branches for bidirectional information flow, where the text-to-image decoder localizes the target based on text queries, and the image-to-text decoder reconstructs erased entity phrases to enhance visual feature representations.}
elements.sent-2411-047: LMQFormer~\cite{shah_lqmformer_2024 enhances segmentation with Gaussian-based fusion, dynamic query focus, and an auxiliary loss to prevent query collapse.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,trim=0 0 0 0,clip]{figure/Overview_Nov14.pdf} \caption{\textbf{Overview of {\archname}}. Our proposed method identifies object queries that produce only instance masks of objects specified in the input prompt. To achieve this, we begin with a set of initial object queries and progressively refine them, utilizing both image and text features to associate each query with a targeted object instance in the image as well as a phrase extracted from the input text.} \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-048: Mask2Former~\cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022 pioneered instance segmentation by leveraging a Transformer-based architecture, applying Hungarian matching~\cite{kuhn_hungarian_1955, carion_end--end_2020 between predicted instances and ground truth based on both class and mask costs.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Building on this, ReferFormer~\cite{wu_language_2022 and VATEX~\cite{nguyen-truong_vision-aware_2024 integrate instance matching in the context of referring expressions, focusing on segmenting a single object per expression.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Both models utilize instance-matching mechanisms to align object query and target object described in the language input, getting precise segmentation in one-to-one scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-051: However, their reliance on binary segmentation limits their performance when handling expressions involving multiple objects or ambiguous descriptions, as they lack the capacity for multi-instance differentiation.
elements.sent-2411-052: Figure \ref{fig:overview summarizes our proposed method, {\archname , which leverages both visual and textual inputs to generate instance-aware segmentation masks.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: We integrate text prompt inputs into this model and guide it to only segment instance masks of objects specified in the input prompt.
elements.sent-2411-054: To achieve this, we begin with a set of $N$ randomly initialized object queries and progressively refine them, utilizing both image and text features to correctly associate each query with a targeted object instance in the image as well as a phrase extracted from the input text.
elements.sent-2411-055: This progressive refinement is conducted via $K$ Phrase-Object Transformer blocks that we introduce, which takes the multi-scale visual features $\mathcal{V}$, text features $T_0$, and object queries $Q_0 \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times C}$ as input, to output a refined text feature $T_K$ and object queries $Q_K$ (Sec.. \ref{sec:transformer ).}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The resulting object queries are used to obtain a set of instance segment masks, each associated with a relevance score.
elements.sent-2411-057: The model is trained to identify and segment only objects relevant to the input prompt via instance supervision (Sec.. \ref{sec:supervision ).}
elements.sent-2411-058: This process is facilitated via our proposed Phrase-Object Alignment loss (Sec.
elements.sent-2411-059: It is important to note that, similar to prior works~\cite{cheng_per-pixel_2021, cheng_masked-attention_2022 , the number of object queries $N$ does not need to equal the actual number of relevant objects.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The instance supervision enables the model to refine only object queries that correspond to relevant instances and enforce unmatched queries to be irrelevant to the input prompt throughout the whole refinement process.
elements.sent-2411-061: The resulting instance predictions are then adaptively combined in the Instance Aggregation module, which weighs each instance based on its relevance score, allowing for precise segmentation without fixed selection (Sec.. \ref{sec:merging ).}
elements.sent-2411-062: Finally, a No-Target Predictor uses both the final object queries $Q_K$ and the enhanced text features $T_K$ to determine if the expression points to any target in the image (Sec.. \ref{sec:no_target ).}
elements.sent-2411-063: If "no-target" is predicted during inference, the final output is an all-zero segmentation mask.
elements.sent-2411-064: To extract the text information, we adopted RoBERTa ~\cite{liu_roberta_2019 to embed the input expression into high-level word features $T_0 \in \mathbb{R ^{L \times C}$, where $C$ and $L$ denotes the number of channels and the length of the expression, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: We use an encoder-decoder architecture to extract the multi-scale pixel features $\mathcal{V = \{V_i \in \mathbb{R ^{C \times H_i \times W_i}\}_{i = 1 ^{4}$ from the input image.}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Here, $H_i$ and $W_i$ denote the height and the width of the feature maps in the $i$-th stage, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-067: As in~\cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022 , these visual features $V_i$ are obtained from the Pixel Decoder~\cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022, zhu_deformable_2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Given a set of randomly initialized object queries along with the extracted text and visual features, we apply multiple blocks of our Phrase-Object Transformer, illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:transformer .}
elements.sent-2411-069: These blocks' purpose is to iteratively integrate visual and textual features into the object queries while simultaneously embedding the information from the object queries into the text features.
elements.sent-2411-070: This bidirectional feature aggregation, guided by our Phrase-Object Alignment loss, enables associating each relevant object instance in the image to an object query and the corresponding text phrase features.
elements.sent-2411-071: More specifically, the $k$-th transformer block takes as input the pixel features $V_k \in \mathbb{R ^{H_k \times W_k \times C}$, text features $T_{k - 1 \in \mathbb{R ^{L \times C}$, and object query $Q_{k - 1 \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times C}$ and outputs refined object query $Q_{k}$ and text features $T_{k}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Similar to Mask2Former~\cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022 , visual features at specific scales are input to different blocks in a round-robin manner and where $V_k$ denotes the visual features input to the $k$-th transformer block.}
elements.sent-2411-073: We employ an Object-Text Cross Attention layer with a bidirectional attention mechanism~\cite{xu_bridging_2023, nguyen-truong_vision-aware_2024, hu_beyond_2023, chng_mask_2024 , allowing both text features and object queries to be transformed based on information from both sides.}
elements.sent-2411-074: To obtain the refined object queries $Q_{k}$, we pass the object queries $Q_{k - 1}$ sequentially through a cross-attention layer with visual features $V_k$, an Object-Text Cross Attention layer with text features $T_{k - 1}$, a self-attention layer, and an FFN layer.
elements.sent-2411-075: Simultaneously, the text features $T_{k - 1}$ are passed through the same Object-Text Cross Attention layer to produce the refined text features $T_{k}$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Previous approaches~\cite{xu_bridging_2023, nguyen-truong_vision-aware_2024, hu_beyond_2023, chng_mask_2024 usually directly enhance the text features by using pixel features.}
elements.sent-2411-077: In contrast, we enhance them by using object queries.
elements.sent-2411-078: This mechanism not only produces a much smaller memory footprint as it avoids computing attention weights with high-resolution pixel feature maps, but it can also produce mutual interaction at the object level instead of the pixel-wise level.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=0 0 0 0,clip]{figure/POT.pdf} \caption{Phrase-Object Transformer. We employ an Object-Text Cross Attention layer with a bidirectional attention mechanism allowing both text features and object queries to be transformed based on information from both sides.} \label{fig:transformer}
elements.sent-2411-079: Our proposed Phrase-Object Alignment (POA) loss, illustrated in Fig. \ref {fig:alignment improves the alignment between object features and the semantics of the text expression, ensuring that each object query aligns closely with the relevant part of the input text.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This step enhances the model's ability to associate specific object instances with language cues, particularly in complex scenarios involving multiple instances.
elements.sent-2411-081: We first derive the phrase-object relevant scores $R_k$ from the affinity between the text features $T_k$ and the object queries $Q_k$.
elements.sent-2411-082: These scores quantify the alignment of each object feature with the text features, effectively weighting the text features by their relevance to the objects.
elements.sent-2411-083: The phrase features $P_k \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times C}$ of $N$ objects are then obtained by the weighted sum of text features using the relevance scores: \begin{equation}R_k = softmax(\frac{Q_kT_k^\top}{\sqrt{C}}) P_k = R_kT_k\end{equation} Then, the alignment loss $\mathcal{L _{phrase (i)$ for the $i$-th object is then calculated based on the similarity between the obtained phrase feature $P_k^i$ of this object and the object query $Q_k^i$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{phrase}(i) = 1 - sim(P_k^i, Q_k^i), \end{equation} where $sim$ denotes the cosine similarity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This loss quantifies how well each object query aligns with the semantic part of the prompt that best describes it.
elements.sent-2411-085: Minimizing this loss, therefore, encourages the model to modify the text features and object queries to strengthen their alignment.
elements.sent-2411-086: This is demonstrated in \cref{fig:alignment_visualization , where it visualizes one object query, its corresponding segment, and its relevant text phrase parsed from the input text (highlighted in red).}
elements.sent-2411-087: In the next section, we describe how we incorporate this alignment loss into our instance segmentation framework.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/alignment.pdf} \caption{Phrase-Object Alignment Loss. Given an object query, we first compute a text feature embedding representing a text phrase that best aligns with the query (highlighted in red). Then, our phrase-object alignment loss penalizes the cosine difference between the object query and this phrase feature.} \label{fig:alignment}
elements.sent-2411-088: After refining the object queries through the Phrase-Object Transformer (POT), our next goal is to produce accurate object instance masks of only objects relevant to the input prompt from these object queries.
elements.sent-2411-089: We supervise this system via instance-aware segmentation losses ~\cite{cheng_per-pixel_2021, cheng_masked-attention_2022 that encourage object queries to output targeted masks, and our proposed POA loss function that strength the association of each object query to a particular textual phrase parsed from the input prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-090: This approach ensures that our model not only segments relevant instances individually but also aligns them semantically with the input expression.. Prediction Heads.
elements.sent-2411-091: Given a refined object query $Q_K \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times C}$, we first predict the probability $\hat{p \in \mathbb{R ^N$ of these instances being related to the expression input via a score prediction head.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Similar, $N$ instance masks $\hat{s \in \mathbb{R ^{\frac{H {4 \times \frac{W {4 \times N}$ associated to this object query can be computed by: \begin{equation}\hat{s} = V_4 \cdot Q_K^\top,\end{equation} where $V_4 \in \mathbb{R ^{\frac{H {4 \times \frac{W {4 \times C}$ is the pixel features at the high-resolution scale extracted from the Pixel Decoder (Sec.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The ground truth segmentation \( \mathcal{M _{gt \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W \) is constructed by combining a set of \( M \) ground-truth instance segments \( s = \{s_i\}_{i = 1 ^{M \).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: To supervise our predictions effectively, we must first establish a one-to-one correspondence between the predicted instances and the ground-truth instances.
elements.sent-2411-095: We achieve this by utilize Hungarian Matching~\cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022, kuhn_hungarian_1955 to find the optimal set of prediction indices \( \omega^* = \{\omega_i\}_{i = 1 ^{M \) that minimizes the following matching cost: \begin{equation}\omega^* = \arg \min_{\omega \subseteq [N]} \sum_{i = 1}^{M} L_{match}(\omega_i, i),\end{equation} where the per-instance matching cost \( \mathcal{L _{\text{match \) for $i$-th predicted instance and $j$-th ground-truth instance is defined as: \begin{equation}L_{{match}}(i, j) = \lambda_{{score}}L_{{score}}(\hat{p}_i, 1) + \lambda_{{mask}}L_{{mask}}(\hat{s}_i, s_j) \notag + \lambda_{{phrase}}L_{{phrase}}(i)\end{equation} Here, \( \mathcal{L _{{score \) is the BCE loss that promotes high confidence for relevant instances, \( \mathcal{L _{{mask \) is the combination of dice loss~\cite{sudre_generalised_2017 and BCE loss, and \( \mathcal{L _{{phrase \) enhances the alignment between the object query with the corresponding expression phrase.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The hyper-parameters \( \lambda_{{score , \lambda_{{mask , \lambda_{{phrase \) control the balance of each component in the matching cost.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: After determining the optimal match, we define the instance loss $\mathcal{L _{inst}$ to optimize the model for both matched and unmatched samples.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The total instance loss is given by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{inst} = \sum^{M}_{i = 1}{\mathcal{L}_{match}(\omega^*_i, i)} + \sum_{i = 1, i \notin \omega^*}^{N}{\mathcal{L}_{score}(\hat{p}_i, 0)},\end{equation} where the first term ensures that for each matched instance, the model minimizes the combined matching loss while the second term penalizes unmatched instances by encouraging their relevance scores \( \hat{p _i \) to approach zero, indicating irrelevance to the expression.}
elements.sent-2411-099: The goal of GRES is to generate a single binary segmentation map of relevant pixels.
elements.sent-2411-100: Given $N$ candidate instances and their corresponding relevant scores, $\{\hat{s _i, \hat{p _i\}_{i = 1 ^{N}$, we aim to properly select and aggregate only instances that correspond to the referring expression.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: A straightforward method is to only select instances with relevance scores exceeding a fixed threshold~\cite{yu_mattnet_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-102: However, this method may yield inconsistent results by either including irrelevant instances or excluding relevant ones that fail to surpass the threshold.
elements.sent-2411-103: Instead, we propose an Adaptive Instance Aggregation (AIA) module as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:adaptive_merging .}
elements.sent-2411-104: This module dynamically adjusts the weighting of instances based on their relevance scores.
elements.sent-2411-105: This allows instances with higher scores to contribute more significantly to the final segmentation, while lower-scored instances are down-weighted, providing a more flexible mechanism for identifying and merging relevant object segments.
elements.sent-2411-106: Additionally, we apply a PReLU activation to the segmentation masks before merging, effectively suppressing negative values associated with irrelevant or background regions.
elements.sent-2411-107: The final merged segmentation mask is computed as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{M}_{merged} = \text{Sigmoid}(\sum_{i = 1}^{N}{(\hat{p}_i \cdot \text{PReLU}(\hat{s}_i))})\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=0 20 0 0,clip]{figure/AdaptiveMerging.pdf} \caption{Adaptive Instance Aggregation. We use $\sigma$ for the PReLU activation.} \label{fig:adaptive_merging}
elements.sent-2411-108: We supervise the merging process, via a mask loss between the final merged mask $\mathcal{M _{merged}$ and ground-truth mask $\mathcal{M _{\text{gt }$. \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{merged} = \mathcal{L}_{mask}(\mathcal{M}_{merged}, \mathcal{M}_{gt}) \end{equation} This loss encourages the final merged mask to closely match the ground truth, effectively guiding both the instance predictions and the adaptive aggregation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In essence, simply selecting masks with scores exceeding a threshold is analogous to a hard-assignment method, while our weighting function represents a soft-assignment approach, providing greater flexibility and robustness in segment merging.
elements.sent-2411-110: We verify its effectiveness in the ablation study (Section \ref{sec:ablation ).}

\multicolumn{1}{l|}{LAVT~\cite{yang_lavt_2022}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 57.64 58.40 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{49.32} 65.32 65.90 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{49.25} 55.04 55.83 48.46 \multicolumn{11}{c}{GRES Methods} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{ReLA~\cite{liu_gres_2023}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 62.42 63.60 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{56.37} 69.26 70.03 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{59.02} 59.88 61.02 58.40 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{LQMFormer~\cite{shah_lqmformer_2024}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 64.98 70.94 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{67.47} - - \multicolumn{1}{c|}{-} - - - \multicolumn{1}{l|}{MABP~\cite{li_bring_2024}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 65.72 68.86 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{62.18} 71.59 72.81 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{-} 62.76 64.04 - \multicolumn{1}{l|}{HDC~\cite{luo_hdc_2024}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 65.42 68.23 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{63.38} 71.60 72.52 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{65.29} 62.79 63.85 60.68 \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{\archname(Ours)} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 68.94 74.34 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{79.72} 73.22 74.51 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{75.65} 63.88 65.74 70.72 \end{tabular} } \caption{Quantitative comparison of LLM-based, RES, and GRES methods across val, testA, and testB sets of gRefCOCO dataset. \textsuperscript{\textdagger} denotes using external datasets.} \label{tab:comparison}
elements.sent-2411-111: No-target is a challenging GRES scenario where the input expression does not refer to any object in the image.
elements.sent-2411-112: Some prior methods formulate this by directly forcing the segmentation prediction into an all-zero case~\cite{hu_beyond_2023, yang_lavt_2022, wang_cris_2022 or using object counting~\cite{luo_hdc_2024 to improve no-target prediction.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Recent works like ReLA~\cite{liu_gres_2023 and LQMFormer~\cite{shah_lqmformer_2024 predict the no-target likelihood by averaging all object queries.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: However, averaging all queries equally can lead to inaccuracies, especially when small target objects are overlooked due to dominant non-target regions.
elements.sent-2411-115: To address this, we propose a method that leverages both object queries and text features and prioritizes object queries based on their relevance scores.
elements.sent-2411-116: Specifically, we compute a weighted sum of instance representations to form a global token $Q_{global \in \mathbb{R ^C$. \begin{equation}Q_{global} = \sum_{i = 1}^{N}{(\hat{p}_i \cdot Q_K^i)}, \end{equation} where $Q_K^i$ is the $i$-th refined object query and $\hat{p _i$ is its relevance score.}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: We also extract a sentence-level text feature $T_{sen \in \mathbb{R ^C$ by averaging word-level features $T_K$: $T_{sen = \text{Average (T_K, \text{dim = 0 ).$ Combining $Q_{global}$ and $T_{sen}$ into a unified representation $Q_{nt}$ provides a comprehensive view that captures both instance and sentence-level context, allowing us to accurately predict the no-target likelihood $\hat{p _{nt}$: \begin{equation}Q_{nt} = Concat(Q_{global}, T_{sen}) \hat{p}_{nt} = MLP(Q_{nt})\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: We supervise our framework with three training objectives: 1) Final mask supervision $\mathcal{L _{merged}$ which is the combination of dice loss~\cite{sudre_generalised_2017 and BCE loss; 2) $\mathcal{L _{instance}$, which guides the association between object queries, targeted object masks, and relevant input text phrases (section \ref{sec:supervision ); and 3) $\mathcal{L _{nt}$, which is a BCE loss measuring the existence of instances~\cite{liu_gres_2023 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Therefore, the total loss can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{L}_{total} = \lambda_{merged}\mathcal{L}_{merged} + \lambda_{inst}\mathcal{L}_{inst} + \lambda_{nt}\mathcal{L}_{nt}, \end{split}\end{equation} where $\lambda_{merged , \lambda_{inst , \lambda_{nt}$ are the scalar coefficients.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Our experiments are conducted on two primary benchmarks in GRES: gRefCOCO~\cite{liu_gres_2023 and Ref-ZOM~\cite{hu_beyond_2023 , which derive images from COCO dataset~\cite{lin_microsoft_2014, yu_modeling_2016, mao_generation_2016 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: We assess \archname{ using standard metrics on the gRefCOCO~\cite{liu_gres_2023 and Ref-ZOM~\cite{hu_beyond_2023 datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: For gRefCOCO, we employ cIoU, gIoU, and N-acc metrics, while for Ref-ZOM, we use mIoU, oIoU, and Acc metrics.
elements.sent-2411-123: Detailed descriptions of these datasets and metrics are provided in the supplementary material.. \parag{Implementation Details.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Our model is implemented in PyTorch.
elements.sent-2411-125: Our model uses Swin-Transformer-B~\cite{liu_swin_2021 as the visual encoder and we adopt RoBERTa~\cite{liu_roberta_2019 as our text encoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: The visual encoder is initialized with classification weights pre-trained on ImageNet22K~\cite{russakovsky_imagenet_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-127: We use $K = 9$ blocks of Phrase-Object Transformer in total.
elements.sent-2411-128: We resize the image input to the resolution of $480 \times 480$ for both training and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-129: We use the AdamW~\cite{loshchilov_decoupled_2018 optimizer with a batch size of 32.}
elements.sent-2411-130: The learning rate is set to $10^{-4}$ and linear decreasing to $10^{-6}$ in 20 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-131: The entire training process takes approximately 24 hours on four NVIDIA A5000 GPUs.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,trim=0 0 0 0,clip]{figure/Alignment_visualization.pdf} \caption{We visualize the segmentation heatmaps of top score queries and the corresponding alignment with the expression. We highlight words that are most aligned with each query. It can be seen that highlighted words can describe the instance it targets.} \label{fig:alignment_visualization}
elements.sent-2411-132: We evaluate {\archname on the gRefCOCO~\cite{liu_gres_2023 and Ref-ZOM~\cite{hu_beyond_2023 datasets, comparing our performance with state-of-the-art methods in both LLM-based, RES and GRES approaches.}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Tables \ref{tab:comparison and \ref{tab:comparison_refzom summarize the results, demonstrating that {\archname (highlighted in \colorbox[HTML]{CBCEFB {lavender ) consistently outperforms existing approaches across all metrics and datasets.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: On the gRefCOCO dataset (Table \ref{tab:comparison ), {\archname achieves a notable improvement in all metrics across all validation and test splits.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Specifically, in the validation set, {\archname attains the highest scores, with $68.94\%$ in cIoU, $74.34\%$ in gIoU, and $79.72\%$ in N-acc.}
elements.sent-2411-136: These scores exceed previous best-performing GRES models, such as LQMFormer \cite{shah_lqmformer_2024 , and significantly outperform RES and LLM-based methods, including LISA-7B \cite{lai_lisa_2024 and GSVA-7B \cite{xia_gsva_2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: For the Ref-ZOM dataset (Table \ref{tab:comparison_refzom ), {\archname also achieves state-of-the-art results, with cIoU, gIoU, and N-acc surpassing all previous methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: In particular, {\archname records a cIoU of $70.8\%$, gIoU of $71.1\%$, and an N-acc of $94.23\%$, demonstrating substantial improvements over both GRES methods like DMMI \cite{hu_beyond_2023 and RES models such as LAVT \cite{yang_lavt_2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: These results confirm that {\archname not only advances the current state of GRES but also sets new benchmarks for segmentation quality across diverse datasets, solidifying its robustness and adaptability in vision-language tasks.}
elements.figure-2411-008: 0.95\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}lcccc@{}} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{\multirow{2}{*}{Method}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{Backbone}} \multicolumn{3}{c}{test} \cmidrule(l){3-5} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{} mIoU oIoU Acc \multicolumn{5}{c}{LLM-based Methods} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{LISA-7B\textsuperscript{\textdagger}~\cite{lai_lisa_2024}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{SAM-ViT-H} 65.39 66.41 93.39 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{GSVA-7B\textsuperscript{\textdagger}~\cite{xia_gsva_2024}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{SAM-ViT-H} 68.13 68.29 94.59 \multicolumn{5}{c}{RES Methods} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{MCN~\cite{luo_multi-task_2020}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ResNet-101} 54.70 55.03 75.81 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{CMPC~\cite{liu_cross-modal_2022}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{DarkNet-53} 55.72 56.19 77.01 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{VLT~\cite{ding_vision-language_2021}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ResNet-101} 60.43 60.21 79.16 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{LAVT~\cite{yang_lavt_2022}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 64.78 64.45 83.11 \multicolumn{5}{c}{GRES Methods} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{DMMI~\cite{hu_beyond_2023}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 68.21 68.77 87.02 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{HDC~\cite{luo_hdc_2024}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 69.31 68.81 93.34 \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{{\archname(Ours)}} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Swin-B} 70.81 71.13 94.23 \end{tabular} } \caption{Quantitative comparison of LLM-based, RES, and GRES methods on Ref-ZOM dataset. \textsuperscript{\textdagger} denotes using external datasets.} \label{tab:comparison_refzom}
elements.figure-2411-010: 0.85\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}lccc@{}} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Setting} cIoU gIoU N-acc \multicolumn{4}{l}{(a) Instance Supervision} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{None} 63.33 66.95 70.56 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Vanilla} 66.26 70.32 76.19 \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Phrase-Aligned} 68.94 74.34 79.72 \multicolumn{4}{l}{(b) Design option of aggregation module} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Fixed selection} 66.67 69.25 72.96 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{AIA w.o PReLU} 68.13 72.35 78.22 \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{AIA} 68.94 74.34 79.72 \multicolumn{4}{l}{(c) No-target Predictor} \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Concat($Q_{global}, T_{sen}$)} 68.94 74.34 79.72 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{$Q_{global}$} 67.85 71.19 70.33 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{$T_{sen}$} 64.29 67.91 75.55 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Average all object queries} 64.01 66.29 69.23 \multicolumn{4}{l}{(d) Number of queries} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{$N = 20$} 67.64 72.67 77.25 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{$N = 50$} 68.56 74.23 79.85 \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{$N = 100$} 68.94 74.34 79.72 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{$N = 200$} 68.01 73.24 78.12 \multicolumn{4}{l}{(e) Transformer Decoder} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Mask2Former~\cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022}} 44.43 47.42 55.58 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{ReLA~\cite{liu_gres_2023}} 67.16 69.23 74.56 \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{POT} 68.94 74.34 79.72 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study on the validation set of gRefCOCO.} \label{tab:ablation_full}
elements.sent-2411-140: We perform several ablation studies to evaluate the effectiveness of the key components in our {\archname model on gRefCOCO~\cite{liu_gres_2023 dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: The results are listed in the Table \ref{tab:ablation_full .}
elements.sent-2411-142: We highlight our default configuration with \colorbox[HTML]{CBCEFB {lavender .. Instance Supervision.}}
elements.sent-2411-143: We examine the effect of different instance supervision methods in Table \ref{tab:ablation_full (a).}
elements.sent-2411-144: Without instance supervision, the model is trained only with the merged mask loss, achieving only 63.33\% cIoU, 66.95\% gIoU, and 70.56\% N-acc.
elements.sent-2411-145: Standard instance supervision without phrase alignment improves cIoU to 66.26\%.
elements.sent-2411-146: However, our proposed Phrase-Aligned Instance Supervision further boosts performance to 68.94\% cIoU, 74.34\% gIoU, and 79.72\% N-acc, demonstrating that incorporating Phrase-Object Alignment during instance matching improves segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-148: In Table \ref{tab:ablation_full (b), we explore various designs for the aggregation module, including fixed threshold instance selection and our AIA module with and without the PReLU activation.}
elements.sent-2411-149: Among these, the AIA with PReLU setting achieves the highest performance.
elements.sent-2411-150: This indicates that adaptive weighting and PReLU activation effectively enhance the merging process.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,trim=0 20 0 0]{figure/Visualization_Nov14.pdf} \caption{Visualization on gRefCOCO dataset. We compare our results with ReLA, which is the previous state-of-the-art method. We also show our high-relevance predicted instances in the last row. Best viewed in color.} \label{fig:visualization}
elements.sent-2411-151: The configuration of the no-target predictor also plays a critical role in segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-152: We compare four configurations in Table \ref{tab:ablation_full (c): using both $Q_{global}$ and sentence features $T_{sen}$, only $Q_{global}$, $T_{sen}$ alone, and averaging the object queries.}
elements.sent-2411-153: The concatenation of global token $Q_{global}$ and $T_{sen}$ outperforms all others, achieving top scores in cIoU, gIoU, and N-acc.
elements.sent-2411-154: This setup captures both the global textual context and instance relevance to handle no-target scenarios effectively.. Number of queries.
elements.sent-2411-155: We investigate the impact of the number of queries in Table \ref{tab:ablation_full (d), testing values of $N \in \{20, 50, 100, 200\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Our results show that $N = 100$ provides the best balance, achieving the highest cIoU, gIoU, and N-acc scores.
elements.sent-2411-157: Increasing the number of queries beyond 100 does not yield further improvement, while fewer queries lead to a drop in performance, suggesting that $100$ queries provide optimal candidate instance coverage.. Transformer Decoder.
elements.sent-2411-158: Finally, we compare our POT with other decoders~\cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022, liu_gres_2023 in Table \ref{tab:ablation_full (e).}}
elements.sent-2411-159: By utilizing the Mask2Former transformer decoder directly without any text information guidance, the model seems to randomly predict instances in the image, achieving 44.43\% in cIoU.
elements.sent-2411-160: Incorporating ReLA~\cite{liu_gres_2023 Decoder enhances the object queries with text features and with the support of the remaining of our architecture, improving the result to 67.17\% in cIoU.}
elements.sent-2411-161: Our POT decoder achieves the best result.
elements.sent-2411-162: This highlights the effectiveness of our transformer design in capturing cross-modal interactions to enhance both object queries and text features so that they can better align with each other.
elements.sent-2411-163: To demonstrate the effectiveness of \archname~in handling complex referring expressions, we present qualitative visualizations comparing our method with the previous state-of-the-art model, ReLA, in Figure \ref{fig:visualization .}
elements.sent-2411-164: In the last row, we show the high-scoring relevant instances predicted by \archname.
elements.sent-2411-165: ReLA, lacking object-level reasoning, frequently over-segments (columns A, B, C, H), under-segments (columns D, G), or exhibits both issues (columns E, F).
elements.sent-2411-166: In contrast, \archname~explicitly identifies relevant instances and effectively combines them into an accurate segmentation map.
elements.sent-2411-167: In column E, for the expression "all the women," \archname~ correctly segments three individual women at the instance level but includes an extra whole-image prediction, likely influenced by the word "all" in a specific query.
elements.sent-2411-168: However, our AIA effectively down-weights this broad instance, integrating relevant lower-scoring predictions to produce a refined segmentation for complex expressions.
elements.sent-2411-169: Our method is not without limitations.
elements.sent-2411-170: Despite its strong performance, \archname{ faces challenges in interpreting hierarchical or compositional relationships between objects and their attributes, particularly in no-target scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Consequently, the model may fail when expressions include attributes partially describing the target object while introducing conflicting information.
elements.sent-2411-172: For example, in the last column of Figure~\ref{fig:visualization , \archname{ correctly segments ``the bowl on the left side" but is distracted by the additional attribute ``with the white soup inside it," resulting in inaccurate segmentation.}}
elements.sent-2411-173: These limitations highlight the need for improved contextual understanding and better handling of relationship dependencies to enhance performance in both complex expressions and no-target situations.
elements.sent-2411-174: In this work, we presented \archname{ , a novel instance-aware model for Generalized Referring Expression Segmentation (GRES) to effectively address complex multi-object scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-175: By adapting instance segmentation frameworks and introducing a Phrase-Object Alignment, \archname{ explicitly identifies and associates individual object instances with specific textual phrases.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Additionally, our Adaptive Instance Aggregation module enhances the integration of relevant instances, ensuring robust performance in complex multi-object and no-target scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-177: Extensive evaluations on the gRefCOCO and Ref-ZOM benchmarks showed that \archname{ outperforms state-of-the-art GRES and LLM-based methods, setting a new standard in the field.}
elements.sent-2411-178: These advances pave the way for more precise and versatile multimodal applications in computer vision such as human-robot interaction and image editing.
elements.sent-2411-179: We then provide more about our implementation details.
elements.sent-2411-180: Finally, we show more analysis of our experiments in both GRES and traditional RES tasks.
elements.sent-2411-181: Proposed by Liu \etal~\cite{liu_gres_2023 , the gRefCOCO dataset consists of 19,994 images of 60,287 distinct instances described by 278,232 language expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-182: These annotations include 80,022 multi-target and 32,202 no-target samples.
elements.sent-2411-183: For evaluation on gRefCOCO dataset, we use the following metrics:.
elements.sent-2411-184: For no-target samples, the per-image IoU is 1 if we predict correctly a no-target sample and 0 in the other cases.
elements.sent-2411-185: N-acc (no-target accuracy): Quantifies the accuracy of no-target identification, i.e., how well the model identifies cases where no target is present.
elements.sent-2411-186: Proposed by Hu\etal~\cite{hu_beyond_2023 , the Ref-ZOM is similar to gRefCOCO, introducing the one-to-one, one-to-many, and one-to-zero referring expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-187: These cases correspond to the single-target, multi-target, and no-target samples in gRefCOCO, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-188: Ref-ZOM dataset consists of 55,078 images of 74,942 annotated instances, which includes 41,842 annotated objects under one-to-many settings, 11,937 one-to-zero samples, and 42,421 one-to-one objects.
elements.sent-2411-189: For Ref-ZOM, gIoU and cIoU are substituted to the equivalent metrics in RES, mIoU and oIoU.
elements.sent-2411-190: However, different from gRefCOCO, mIoU and gIoU only count for one-to-one and one-to-many samples.
elements.sent-2411-191: For one-to-zero samples, we use the accuracy (acc) metric, which measures the classification performance on empty-target expressions.
elements.sent-2411-192: While the metrics to measure the performance for GRES task are based on the binary ground-truth mask, both gRefCOCO~\cite{liu_gres_2023 and Ref-ZOM~\cite{hu_beyond_2023 datasets provide instance mask annotations for each expression.}}
elements.sent-2411-193: We utilize these annotations for our instance supervision.
elements.sent-2411-194: Our model is optimized using AdamW~\cite{loshchilov_decoupled_2018 optimizer with the initial learning rate of $10^{-4}$ and linear decreasing to $10^{-6}$ after 20 epochs.}
elements.sent-2411-195: For instance matching, the coefficients are set as $\lambda_{score = 2, \lambda_{mask = 5, \lambda_{phrase = 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-196: In the loss function, we set $\lambda_{merged = 5, \lambda_{inst = 1, \text{ and \lambda_{nt = 0.1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: RoBERTa-base~\cite{liu_roberta_2019 is used as the Text Encoder to extract language features and the Pixel Decoder comprises 6 layers of Deformable Transformer, following Mask2Former~\cite{cheng_masked-attention_2022 .}}
elements.figure-2411-012: 0.7\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}lccccccc@{}} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{One-to-One} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{One-to-Many} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Overall Targets} \multicolumn{1}{c}{One-to-Zero} \cmidrule(l){2-8} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{\multirow{-2}{*}{Method}} oIoU \multicolumn{1}{c|}{mIoU} oIoU \multicolumn{1}{c|}{mIoU} oIoU \multicolumn{1}{c|}{mIoU} Acc \multicolumn{1}{l|}{MCN~\cite{luo_multi-task_2020}} 52.09 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{53.14} 58.04 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{57.21} 55.05 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{54.70} 75.81 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{CMPC~\cite{liu_cross-modal_2022}} 52.46 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{52.89} 60.23 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{60.27} 56.19 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{55.72} 77.01 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{VLT~\cite{ding_vision-language_2021}} 59.07 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{58.96} 61.42 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{62.79} 60.21 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{60.43} 79.26 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{LAVT~\cite{yang_lavt_2022}} 63.21 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{64.56} 65.69 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{65.14} 64.45 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{64.78} 83.11 \multicolumn{1}{l|}{DMMI~\cite{hu_beyond_2023}} 65.43 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{66.83} 72.20 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{70.44} 68.77 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{68.21} 87.02 \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{\archname(Ours)} 66.36 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{68.41} 76.77 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{75.09} 71.13 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{70.81} 94.23 \end{tabular} } \caption{Quantitative comparison across 3 cases of Ref-ZOM dataset.} \label{tab:ref_zom_analysis}
elements.sent-2411-198: Table 1 showcases the results of our method compared to state-of-the-art methods across one-to-one, one-to-many, and one-to-zero cases in the Ref-ZOM dataset.
elements.sent-2411-199: Our model demonstrates significant improvements across all scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-200: In one-to-one cases, \archname{ surpasses prior SOTA with a margin of 0.93\% and 2.58\% in terms of oIoU and mIoU, respectively, showcasing our precision in identifying individual objects.}
elements.sent-2411-201: In one-to-many cases, our model outperforms DMMI~\cite{hu_beyond_2023 with a large margin of 4.47\% and 4.65\% in oIoU and mIoU.}
elements.sent-2411-202: For the no-target scenario, the Acc score of $94.23\%$ indicates our model's ability to accurately determine when no valid target is present compared to previous approaches.
elements.sent-2411-203: These results highlight the effectiveness of explicitly predicting relevant instances and adaptive instance aggregation, enabling accurate segmentation for complex expressions.
elements.figure-2411-013: 0.97\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lccccccccccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Method} \multirow{2}{*}{Backbone} \multicolumn{3}{c}{RefCOCO} \multicolumn{3}{c}{RefCOCO+} \multicolumn{3}{c}{RefCOCOg} \cmidrule(lr){3-5} \cmidrule(lr){6-8} \cmidrule(lr){9-10} val test A test B val test A test B val test VLT \cite{ding_vision-language_2021} Darknet-53 65.65 68.29 62.73 55.50 59.20 49.36 52.99 56.65 ReSTR \cite{kim_restr_2022} ViT-B/16 67.22 69.30 64.45 55.78 60.44 48.27 - - CRIS \cite{wang_cris_2022} ResNet-101 70.47 73.18 66.10 62.27 68.08 53.68 59.87 60.36 LAVT \cite{yang_lavt_2022} Swin-B 72.73 75.82 68.79 62.14 68.38 55.10 61.24 62.09 ReLA \cite{liu_gres_2023} Swin-B 73.82 76.48 70.18 66.04 71.02 57.65 65.00 65.97 DMMI \cite{hu_beyond_2023} Swin-B 74.14 77.13 70.16 63.98 69.73 57.03 61.98 63.46 LQMFormer \cite{shah_lqmformer_2024} Swin-B 74.16 76.82 71.04 65.91 71.84 57.59 64.73 66.04 CGFormer \cite{tang_contrastive_2023} Swin-B 74.75 77.30 70.64 64.54 71.00 57.14 62.51 64.68 PolyFormer$^\dagger$ \cite{liu_polyformer_2023} Swin-B 74.82 76.64 71.06 67.64 72.89 59.33 67.76 69.05 LISA-7B$^\dagger$ \cite{lai_lisa_2024} SAM-ViT-H 74.1 76.5 71.1 62.4 67.4 56.5 66.4 68.5 GSVA-7B$^\dagger$ \cite{xia_gsva_2024} SAM-ViT-H 77.2 78.9 73.5 65.9 69.6 59.8 72.7 73.3 Prompt-RIS$^\dagger$ \cite{shang_prompt-driven_2024} SAM-ViT-B/16 76.36 80.37 72.29 67.06 73.58 58.96 64.79 67.16 MagNet$^\ddagger$~\cite{chng_mask_2024} Swin-B {76.55} {78.27} {72.15} {68.10} \textbf{{73.64}} 61.81 {67.79} {69.29} \rowcolor[HTML]{CBCEFB} \archname{}(Ours)$^\ddagger$ Swin-B 78.05 {80.02} 75.03 68.32 {72.85} {60.15} 73.47 75.73 \end{tabular} } \caption{Comparison with SOTA methods in RES task using the oIoU metric. $^\ddagger$ indicates combining the train splits from these 3 datasets with test images removed to prevent data leakage. $^\dagger$ indicates using additional data beyond RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, and G-Ref.} \label{tab:sota-comparison}
elements.sent-2411-204: While our primary focus is addressing multi-target scenarios in Generalized Referring Expression Segmentation, we also evaluate our model on the traditional RES task to provide a broader performance perspective.
elements.sent-2411-205: We follow MagNet~\cite{chng_mask_2024 to combine the train splits from 3 datasets RefCOCO, RefCOCO+~\cite{kazemzadeh_referitgame_2014 , and RefCOCOg~\cite{mao_generation_2016 with test images removed to prevent data leakage.}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: As shown in Table 2, while \archname{ is designed for GRES, it also performs competitively on traditional RES benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Notably, on the challenging RefCOCOg dataset, \archname{ surpasses the previous SOTA by significant margins of $5.68\%$ and $6.44\%$ in validation and test splits, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-208: This indicates that our instance-aware reasoning and phrase-object alignment are beneficial even in traditional RES, suggesting broader applicability.
